topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the final proof. Another Tuesday, another soul-crushing cycle of editing and submission. He leaned back in his worn office chair, the springs groaning a familiar protest under his weight. The lukewarm coffee in his mug had long since cooled to tepid indifference. He clicked the link to the published piece, a flicker of something—annoyance, perhaps—crossing his face as he scanned the headline. The words swam, then settled. A stranger’s name. His carefully crafted analysis, his meticulous research, was attributed to someone else. Elias let out a slow, almost inaudible sigh, running a hand over his tired eyes. He felt a distinct tightening in his chest, a dull ache of profound anticlimax. He started clicking through the comments section, a restless energy bubbling beneath his skin, searching for any sign of absurdity, any hint that this was some elaborate, ridiculous prank.

[story 2]

Sarah traced the edges of the printed article with a fingertip, the glossy paper feeling offensively smooth under her touch. The afternoon light slanted across her desk, illuminating dust motes dancing in the stale air. She’d been waiting for this moment, some kind of dramatic climax to the monotony of her week, and instead, she found a flat, cold realization. Her words, her voice, were not the ones attached to this publication. A slow, heavy slump settled into her posture. She picked up her phone, the screen illuminating a landscape of endless, uninteresting notifications. Her jaw tightened, a small, involuntary clenching of the muscles. "Seriously?" she murmured to the empty room, the sound thin and devoid of inflection. She began methodically deleting the draft files, the motions feeling mechanical, devoid of any real investment.

[story 3]

Mark tapped his pen against the desk rhythmically, the sound sharp and irritating in the quiet workspace. He’d been cycling through his inbox for hours, the same dull routine repeating itself—reply, draft, review, repeat. When the email arrived, the subject line was innocuous, but the body of the text hit him like a physical blow. His breakthrough essay, the one he’d poured weeks into, was credited to an unknown author. Mark stopped tapping. His hand froze mid-air. A strange numbness spread through his limbs, making it difficult to move. He leaned forward, resting his elbows on the desk, his gaze fixed on the text, his expression utterly blank. He let his head drop back against the chair, a heavy surrender to the sheer pointlessness of the discovery.
